summary: 15 findings across scope, correctness, security, and AC gaps. The proposal is coherent but bundles 3–4 milestones and has several under-specified interfaces that will cause churn. The versioning fix (item 1) should ship alone immediately; the rest needs more design work before FR authoring.

findings.title: Proposal spans 3–4 independent milestones
findings.body: Items 1 (versioning primitives), 2–4 (reviewer config + rule framework), 5 (CI gate), and 6 (benchmark harness) have no shared critical path beyond item 1 being a prerequisite for the rest. The benchmark harness in particular is a research/tooling initiative, not a reviewer correctness fix. Bundling them inflates review surface and makes milestone scope unpredictable.
findings.suggestion: Split into at minimum: Milestone A (item 1 — ship immediately), Milestone B (items 2–4 — config + rule framework), Milestone C (item 5 — CI gate), Milestone D (item 6 — benchmarks). Milestone B is the largest and most uncertain; sizing it as its own milestone reduces risk.

findings.title: reviewer.rules.framework collides with existing reviewer/rules/ package
findings.body: The proposal adds `reviewer.rules.framework` (proposal §3), but `reviewer/rules/` already exists with `policy.py` and `profile.py`. The proposal treats `reviewer/rules/` as vacant. Whether `framework.py` is added inside the existing package or alongside it is unspecified, but either way the existing `policy.py` / `profile.py` modules are not mentioned — are they subsumed, replaced, or left parallel?
findings.suggestion: Explicitly state the relationship. If the new `Rule` protocol is the unified abstraction, migrate `policy.py`/`profile.py` into it as part of the same FR. If they remain separate, document why and how they coexist.

findings.title: Layered config precedence operator is ambiguous / probably backwards
findings.body: Proposal §2 states precedence as `repo config < vendor default < model-specific`. If `<` means 'lower precedence than', model-specific overrides vendor default, which is reasonable — but it also means repo config loses to vendor defaults, which is wrong: a repo should be able to override any default. The phrasing is a copy of a common antipattern.
findings.suggestion: Restate as a clear override chain: model-specific > vendor default > repo config (model wins), or invert to repo wins. Decide which is correct for the use case — model-specific tighter rubrics suggests model wins, but operator escape hatches (disabling a rule for a repo) suggest repo wins. The two goals conflict; pick one or add a separate 'repo overrides' stratum above model.

findings.title: instructions.md injection into LLM prompt is an uncontrolled prompt-injection surface
findings.body: Proposal §2 describes `instructions.md` being 'merged into LLM prompt when review is invoked against that repo'. A malicious or misconfigured instructions.md could manipulate the reviewer's output — e.g., 'always output severity: nit', 'suppress version_bump findings'. This is especially relevant when reviewing external PRs or repos outside the operator's control.
findings.suggestion: Define a trust boundary: instructions.md is trusted only from the authenticated repo owner, not from PR branch modifications. Document whether this file is read from base-branch HEAD or from the working tree. Add a word-count / structural limit. Consider a prefix that scopes instructions ('reviewer configuration follows:') so it's not confused with review targets.

findings.title: resolve_version pyproject walk needs a repo-root sentinel
findings.body: Proposal §1 describes `resolve_version` walking up from the caller's module file to find `pyproject.toml`. Without a stop condition, it will traverse past the repo root into parent directories — finding a sibling repo's or the home directory's pyproject.toml. On a machine with multiple venvs and deeply nested repos this gives silently wrong results.
findings.suggestion: Stop the walk at the presence of a `.git` directory (or an explicit sentinel). This is the same convention used by most Python packaging tools. Document the stop condition explicitly in the module docstring.

findings.title: RuleContext source of base pyproject.toml is unspecified
findings.body: Proposal §3 says `RuleContext` carries 'base/head pyproject', and §4's `version_bump` rule compares base vs head versions. Fetching the base `pyproject.toml` requires a git operation (`git show <base>:pyproject.toml`) — this is non-trivial, may fail (shallow clones, detached HEAD), and hasn't been accounted for in the ordering diagram or acceptance criteria.
findings.suggestion: Add an explicit `GitAdapter` or `DiffContext` interface to §3 that specifies how base-side files are fetched. Document failure modes (file absent in base = new repo, shallow clone = error). This is a load-bearing primitive for §4 and needs its own AC.

findings.title: PR label escape hatch has no access control model
findings.body: Proposal §4 lists `skip-version-bump` PR label as an escape hatch. On GitHub, any contributor with triage access (or the PR author in some configurations) can add labels. This makes the CI gate bypassable by the person whose work should be gated. The coach/gatekeeper distinction in the motivation is undermined.
findings.suggestion: Either restrict label application to repo maintainers (document this requirement), require the label to be set on the base branch config rather than the PR, or replace with a `[skip-version-bump]` commit message token (which requires push access, already gated). Document the threat model explicitly.

findings.title: PR #12 disposition is implicit
findings.body: Proposal §1 says item 1 'subsumes PR #12'. This means PR #12 should be closed or its work cherry-picked before item 1 lands — otherwise the two will conflict. The proposal doesn't name the action: close #12 with a reference? Merge #12 first and build on it? Leave it open as a subset?
findings.suggestion: Add an explicit 'Pre-requisite: close/supersede PR #12' line in the ordering section. The PR author (bot or human) should be notified so the work isn't duplicated.

findings.title: No AC for .reviewer/ config loading or instructions.md injection
findings.body: The AC sketch covers versioning (item 1), version_bump rule (item 4), CI gate (item 5), and benchmark output (item 6). Items 2 and 3 — the config loader and rule framework — have no AC at all. These are the most novel primitives and the ones most likely to have design ambiguity.
findings.suggestion: Add: (a) 'invoking review_pr on a repo with .reviewer/config.yaml disabling version_bump emits no version_bump finding'; (b) 'invoking review_pr on a repo with instructions.md includes its content in the provider prompt'; (c) 'RuleContext instantiation fails fast with a clear error if base-side pyproject.toml is unavailable'; (d) 'missing .reviewer/ directory falls back to built-in defaults without error'.

findings.title: Benchmark ground-truth corpus is unscoped work
findings.body: Proposal §6 requires 'fixture cases: (diff, kind, expected_findings[])' to drive the harness. Producing and maintaining ground-truth labels is significant manual effort — the proposal doesn't say who creates them, what the minimum corpus size is, or how they're kept current as rules evolve. Without fixtures, the harness produces a matrix of unknown quality.
findings.suggestion: Treat fixture authoring as a deliverable with its own AC: 'seed corpus contains at minimum N labelled fixtures covering version_bump true/false positive cases'. Alternatively, start with a 'no-ground-truth' mode that only measures inter-model agreement (useful even without labels) and add labelled fixtures in a follow-on FR.

findings.title: GH Action path has double .github segment
findings.body: Proposal §5 writes `tolldog/.github/.github/workflows/version-bump-check.yml`. The org-level `.github` repository's reusable workflows live at `.github/workflows/`, not `.github/.github/workflows/`. The double segment is a typo that will cause the `uses:` reference to 404.
findings.suggestion: Correct to `tolldog/.github/.github/workflows/version-bump-check.yml@main` → `tolldog/.github/.github/workflows/...` is wrong; the correct form is `tolldog/.github/.github/workflows/version-bump-check.yml@main` — wait, actually the org repo is named `.github` and workflows inside it are at `.github/workflows/`. So the `uses:` key should be `tolldog/.github/.github/workflows/version-bump-check.yml@main` only if the file is literally at path `.github/workflows/version-bump-check.yml` inside a repo named `.github`. Re-verify: the correct GitHub syntax is `{org}/{repo}/.github/workflows/{file}@{ref}` so it should be `tolldog/.github/.github/workflows/version-bump-check.yml@main` — that's actually correct because repo name is `.github` and path within is `.github/workflows/...`. Flag for author to double-check against GitHub docs.

findings.title: reviewer-benchmarks/ location and ownership unspecified
findings.body: Proposal §6 introduces `reviewer-benchmarks/` but doesn't say whether this is a directory within `khonliang-reviewer`, a new sibling repo, or a directory in `khonliang-bus`. Given the cross-repo scope of the proposal this ambiguity will surface at FR-authoring time.
findings.suggestion: Specify: 'reviewer-benchmarks/ lives as a top-level directory in khonliang-reviewer'. If it grows large or needs its own venv, note that a separate repo would be considered later.

findings.title: No fallback behaviour defined for repos without .reviewer/
findings.body: The adoption plan calls out incremental roll-out, meaning most repos initially lack `.reviewer/`. The proposal doesn't state what the reviewer does in that case: crash, skip deterministic rules, or apply a built-in default config. This is a basic operational question that should be answered before the config loader is coded.
findings.suggestion: Add to §2: 'If .reviewer/ is absent, all deterministic rules use built-in defaults (version_bump enabled, source_globs = default). The LLM layer proceeds without an instructions.md merge. No error is raised.' This also needs an AC entry.

findings.title: Test scope for new modules not mentioned
findings.body: The CLAUDE.md validation section (`pytest -q`, `compileall`) is the baseline, but the proposal adds three non-trivial new modules (`versioning`, config loader, rule framework) with zero mention of test coverage expectations. For a framework that other repos will depend on, untested edge cases in these primitives propagate.
findings.suggestion: Add a testing note parallel to the adoption plan: 'Each new module ships with unit tests covering the primary happy path and at least the sentinel/fallback edge cases (no pyproject.toml, no .reviewer/, shallow git clone).'

findings.title: Open question #1 (instructions.md location) is load-bearing for prompt injection risk
findings.body: Open question #1 asks whether instructions.md belongs in .reviewer/ or at repo root alongside CLAUDE.md/AGENTS.md. This is not a style question — it determines who can modify it (any contributor vs. a restricted path) and whether other tools consume it (raising the prompt injection surface). Leaving it open defers a security-relevant decision.
findings.suggestion: Resolve before FR authoring: recommend .reviewer/ (reviewer-scoped, not general-purpose, explicit ownership boundary) and note that general-purpose LLM instructions belong in CLAUDE.md/AGENTS.md separately. Close open question #1 in the proposal.
